title: Replace Mode
subtitle: R — overwrite as you type. r{c} — overwrite one character.
summary: R enters Replace mode: each typed key overwrites instead of inserting. r{c} overwrites a single character without entering Replace mode.

blocks.text: Insert mode pushes characters around: typing in the middle of a word *grows* the word. Replace mode is different — each typed character *overwrites* the one under the cursor, then advances. Typing 5 characters in Replace mode replaces 5 existing characters.

blocks.label: Replace one char with x (stays in Normal)

blocks.title: Replace Is a First-Class Mode
blocks.text:
Replace mode (`-- REPLACE --`) sits alongside Insert as a top-level mode. The status line shows the change. {key:Esc} exits.

Key difference from Insert: each typed character does a *delete + insert* on the character under the cursor (instead of just an insert). Backspace in Replace mode *restores* the original character (not deletes it back to nothing).

There's also {key:g}{key:R}, **virtual replace mode** — same idea, but newlines and tabs are *not* overwritten with literal characters that would push text around. {key:g}{key:R} treats Tab as a virtual span of spaces; replacing a single column inside the tab keeps the tab character intact. Useful in tab-aligned text.

Replace mode is rarely the right tool for code editing — most people overwrite a region with {key:c}{motion} or {key:c}{textobject}. But for fixed-width formats or quick column overwrites, it's perfect.
{key:r} is one-shot: replace one character, stay in Normal. {key:R} is continuous: enter the mode, overwrite as long as you keep typing. Pick based on how many you have to fix.
